title: practical english usage - Part 213
content:
London was the place where Jake spent Tuesday.

- Jake spent Tuesday IN LONDON.

We   can use these expressions to emphasise a place, time or reason.

2 the    place      where       .. the    day     when      .. the    reason        why     ...

For more information about u/hɑt-dauses, ►    236.

What we want is/are some of those cakes.

noun in an informal style.

it is followed by is!was. But a plural verb is sometimes possible before a plural A  what-ciause is normally considered to be singular; if it begins a cleft sentence Information StructureSection 25 grammar •  275  ellipsis (leaving words out): introduction ‘ Which shoes are you going to wear? ‘ These.’ (= These shoes.) Philharmonic Orchestra.) We're going to hear the London Philharmonic tonight. (= ... the London My car isn't working. I'll have to use Emily's. (= . .. Emily’ s car.) ‘ Do you want large eggs? ‘ No, I'll have small.' (= ... small eggs.) determiners. For details, ►    278.

It is sometimes possible to drop nouns after adjectives, noun modifiers and/or 4 at  the   end   of  a  noun    phrase Doesn't know what she's talking about. (= She doesn’ t...) Seen Lucy? (= Have you seen Lucy?) sentence, if the meaning is clear. For details, ►    277.

In informal speech, unstressed words are often dropped at the beginning of a 3 at  the   beginning        of  a  sentence She was poor but happy. (= ... but she was happy.) a knife and fork (= a knife and a fork) Repeated words are often dropped in co-ordinate structures (►  276).

2 structures        with    and,     but    and   or this evening.) ‘ She's out this evening? ‘ Yes, working.’ (More likely than ‘ Yes, she’s working three chairs’ .) ‘ How many chairs do you need? ‘ Three.' (More likely than ‘ I need ‘ Who said that? ‘ lack.' (More likely than ‘ Jack said that.) about ten'.) ‘ What time are you coming?' ‘ About ten.' (More likely than ‘ I'm coming In replies we  usually avoid repeating information that has just been given.

meaning can be understood without them. This is called ‘ ellipsis’ .

We   often leave out words to avoid repetition, or in other cases when the 275 ellipsis     (leaving     words     out):  introduction in sentences, ►    267.

For more general information about sentence structure and the arrangement of information For more about question-word clauses, ►    266.

That's where we live, (   There's where we live.) - We live there.

This is where you pay. (   Here is where you pay.) - You pay here.

At the beginning of a cleft sentence, this and that often replace emphasised here It was only when I read her email that I realised what was happening.

It was not until I met you that I knew real happiness.

when ...

Time  expressions can be emphasised with It was not until. . . and It was only grammar •  275  ellipsis (leaving words out): introduction Start when ready. (= ... when you are ready.)                                                  -> especially in a formal style.

Subject pronouns with forms of be can be left out after certain conjunctions, 11   be   after    conjunctions (= ... that is available.) It will not be possible to finish the job in the time available.

Who's the girl dancing with your brother? (= ... who  is dancing ...) participles, or adjectives such as available, possible. For details, ►    237.11.

We   can sometimes leave out a relative pronoun and the verb be before 10 reduced      relative      structures:       the    tickets       available,          etc This is the restaurant (which) I was talking about.

I knew (that) she didn't want to help me.

relative pronouns can also be dropped (►  234.4).

In an informal style, the conjunction that is often dropped (►  265); object 9 that     and   relative      pronouns Become a successful writer. This book shows you how.

know who  has been stealing our flowers.) Somebody has been stealing our flowers, but I don't know who. (= ... I don't Clauses can be dropped after question words.

8  question-word          clauses For missing subject or object after as and than (e.g. as was expected), ►    256.
